home.heroDesc: An in-depth guide to the Milvus milvus — exploring its life-cycles, soaring mastery, and the remarkable world of the Red Kite.
home.aboutDesc: The Red Kite is one of Europe's most elegant raptors, instantly recognisable by its deeply forked tail and rust-red plumage. Once nearly extinct in Britain, it has made a remarkable comeback through dedicated reintroduction programmes.
home.maleDesc: Slightly smaller than the female, with a pale grey head and striking rufous-red upperparts. The male's agile flight displays are key to courtship rituals.
home.femaleDesc: Larger and heavier than the male, the female shares similar plumage but tends to be darker overall. She is the primary incubator of the eggs.
home.mapAttribution: Distribution map for Red Kite Milvus milvus.

hatch.heroDesc: After 31-32 days of incubation, the eggs begin to crack. The Red Kite chick uses its egg tooth to break free from its calcified prison.
hatch.struggleP1: The hatching process for Red Kite chicks is a gruelling ordeal that can last over 24 hours. The chick must systematically weaken the shell while conserving enough energy to survive the critical first hours outside.
hatch.struggleP2: Unlike kestrels, Red Kite clutches are smaller, typically 1-3 eggs, but the investment per chick is greater. The parents' attentive brooding is essential for the vulnerable, down-covered hatchlings.
hatch.didYouKnowBody: Red Kites are notorious nest decorators. They adorn their nests with scraps of cloth, plastic, paper, and other found objects — a behaviour unique among European raptors.
hatch.protoptileDownDesc: A covering of pale, fluffy down that insulates the chick during its first days in the exposed tree-top nest.
hatch.closingQuote: "From the canopy's edge, a new story begins — one written in feathers the colour of autumn."

growth.subtitle: Growth and feeding
growth.introParagraph: Red Kite nestlings grow rapidly, fed by both parents who bring a varied diet of carrion, small mammals, and earthworms to the nest. The fledging period lasts approximately 48-50 days.
growth.quote: The parents tear food into small pieces, carefully feeding each chick in turn to ensure even growth across the brood.
growth.secondParagraph: As juveniles develop their distinctive forked tail and rufous plumage, they begin exercising their wings at the nest edge, preparing for their first flight into the world below.
growth.detailCardBody: The distinctive forked tail begins to emerge at around 3 weeks, becoming the Red Kite's most recognisable feature and a crucial tool for its acrobatic flight.
growth.bentoQuote: The young kite watches the world from its tree-top vantage point, instinct already calling it skyward.
growth.dietBody: Red Kite chicks are fed a mixed diet of carrion, small mammals, and invertebrates — reflecting the species' opportunistic feeding habits.

flight.title: Taking Flight
flight.s1Body: A young Red Kite stands at the edge of its tree-top nest, the landscape spreading out below in a patchwork of fields and woodland. This is the moment where instinct meets courage.
flight.fieldNotesQuote: The Red Kite does not merely fly — it dances with the air currents, its forked tail steering with surgical precision.
flight.s2Body: The Red Kite is a supreme soarer, riding thermals for hours with minimal wing-beats. Its deeply forked tail acts as a rudder, allowing constant micro-adjustments in flight.
flight.stat1Desc: One of the largest wingspans of European raptors.
flight.stat3Desc: Exceptional eyesight for spotting carrion from height.
flight.stat4Desc: Speed during controlled descent to food.
flight.s3Body: While British Red Kites are largely resident, populations in northern and eastern Europe migrate south to warmer regions in winter, covering hundreds of kilometres.
flight.didYouKnowBody: Red Kites were once so common in medieval London that they were protected by royal decree for their role as street cleaners, scavenging waste from the streets.
flight.closingQuote: A fork-tailed silhouette against the sky — the Red Kite's flight is poetry written in thermals.
flight.videoDesc: Watch the Red Kite's graceful soaring and acrobatic manoeuvres.

hunt.specimenDesc: An opportunistic feeder, the Red Kite patrols the landscape with keen eyes, equally comfortable taking live prey or scavenging carrion.
hunt.heroParagraph1: The Red Kite is primarily a scavenger, though it also takes live prey when the opportunity arises.
hunt.heroParagraph2: Its hunting technique combines soaring surveillance with quick, agile swoops. The forked tail allows rapid changes of direction, making it surprisingly effective at catching small mammals and birds.
hunt.uvVisionDesc: Red Kites possess extraordinarily sharp vision, capable of spotting a small rodent or piece of carrion from heights of over 100 metres while soaring on thermals.
hunt.talonsDesc: While less powerful than those of dedicated hunters like eagles, the Red Kite's talons are perfectly adapted for snatching food from the ground in a low, sweeping pass.
hunt.volesDesc: The primary food source — roadkill and livestock remains.
hunt.beetlesDesc: A surprisingly important food source, especially in winter.
hunt.birdsDesc: Voles, mice, and young rabbits taken opportunistically.
hunt.othersDesc: Scraps, invertebrates, and occasionally small birds.
hunt.closingQuote: The Red Kite is nature's recycler — an elegant scavenger that turns death into flight.
hunt.videoDesc: Watch the Red Kite's graceful hunting technique in the wild.

mate.heroDesc: Red Kites form long-term pair bonds, returning to the same nesting territory year after year. Their aerial courtship displays are among the most spectacular of any European raptor.
mate.selectionDesc: Courtship involves breathtaking aerial displays where pairs lock talons and spiral downward together. The male also brings gifts of food and nest material to reinforce the bond.
mate.eggsDesc: Laid at 3-day intervals, incubated for 31-32 days primarily by the female.
mate.archiveDesc: Red Kite courtship displays are dramatic and energetic. The talon-grappling spiral dive is believed to test the strength and agility of both partners.
mate.quote: "The Red Kite does not merely pair — it commits to a partnership forged in the sky."
mate.plumageDesc: The rufous-red body plumage and pale, streaked head are key identifiers. During courtship, the forked tail is fanned to its fullest extent.
mate.sanctuaryDesc: Red Kites build large stick nests in the forks of tall deciduous trees, often reusing and adding to the same nest over many years.
mate.audioRecordingSuffix: — explore more Red Kite recordings there.

nest.heroDesc: The Red Kite builds a substantial stick nest high in the fork of a tall tree. Unlike the kestrel, it is a dedicated nest builder, adding material year after year.
nest.borrowedP1: Red Kites construct large nests of sticks and twigs, typically placed in the main fork of a mature deciduous tree at heights of 12-20 metres. The nest is lined with wool, grass, and famously — scraps of rubbish.
nest.borrowedP2: Pairs often return to the same nest year after year, adding fresh material each spring. Over time, nests can grow to impressive sizes, becoming prominent features of the woodland canopy.
nest.didYouKnowBody: Red Kites are famous for decorating their nests with human-made objects. Researchers have found children's toys, underwear, crisp packets, and even banknotes woven into Red Kite nests.
nest.territoryDefenseDesc: Red Kites defend their nesting territory with aerial displays and vocalisations, though they are generally less aggressive than other raptors and may tolerate nearby pairs.
nest.closingQuote: "The Red Kite's nest is not merely a structure — it is a chronicle, growing richer with each passing year."

featureGrid.chaptersDesc: Six stages of a singular existence, from hatching to the next generation.
featureGrid.hatchDesc: From the first crack of the shell to the initial days in the tree-top nest.
featureGrid.growthDesc: The transformation from downy hatchling to fork-tailed fledgling.
featureGrid.flightDesc: Mastering the thermals with the signature forked tail.
featureGrid.huntDesc: The art of scavenging and opportunistic hunting.
featureGrid.mateDesc: Spectacular aerial displays and long-term pair bonds.
featureGrid.nestDesc: Building and decorating the tree-top home.
